episodes.title: The cyber model ships, and the bottleneck moves to memory
episodes.summary: OpenAI launches Daybreak and a GPT-5.5-Cyber vulnerability model weeks after a rival's cyber-capable model was recalled; a new paper reframes prompt injection as a confused-deputy 'role confusion' problem (de-styling cut attack success 61%->10%); and big money moves into the hardware layer with Micron-Anthropic co-designing memory architecture and SpaceX renting GB300 compute to Reflection AI for $150M/month.
topics: EnterpriseClawBench: enterprise agent benchmark built from real workplace sessions; scores harness-model combinations, not model names
PlanBench-XL: long-horizon tool-use planning over 1,665 tools / 327 retail tasks; GPT-5.4 collapses 51.9%->11.4% under blocking, especially when failures have no explicit error signal
PP-OCRv6: three-tier multilingual OCR (1.5M-34.5M params), medium 86.2% detection / 83.2% recognition, +4.6/+5.1pp over v5_server, 50 languages, edge-deployable
Prompt injection as 'role confusion' (Willison; Ye/Cui/Hadfield-Menell): models trust text style over content; de-styling cut attack success 61%->10%; the classic confused-deputy problem

OpenAI Daybreak: GPT-5.5-Cyber vuln-finding/patching model + Codex Security + Patch the Planet (with Trail of Bits) for OSS maintainers; ships the cyber capability the Fable/Mythos recall called too dangerous
Hugging Face local PR triage: 26B Gemma / 35B Qwen one-shot classify GitHub issues/PRs locally, no fine-tuning, via agentic framework + read-only reposhell

Micron-Anthropic strategic agreement: co-designed memory/storage architecture, supply deal, Series H investment, internal Claude deployment; memory bandwidth as the binding constraint on token economics
SpaceX-Reflection AI compute deal: $150M/month (~$1.8B/yr) for Nvidia GB300 at Colossus 2 near Memphis, July 2026 through 2029; open-weights lab renting concentrated compute
GLM-5.2 (Z.ai): first open-weight model that 'feels right in coding harnesses as a general agent' (Interconnects/Lambert)

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: Reframed at the conceptual level: prompt injection named as a confused-deputy 'role confusion' problem, with a concrete fix (de-styling) cutting attack success 61%->10%.
threads.arc: From containment-and-incentives engineering toward a precise mechanism: the model authenticates the format of an instruction, not its source — old security theory (confused deputy) renamed for LLMs.

threads.name: AI quality and verification failures
threads.status: EnterpriseClawBench builds an eval from real proprietary workplace sessions and scores harness-model pairs, not model names.
threads.arc: The eval-honesty beat sharpens again: after benchmarks that only measured Python and leaderboards that don't transfer, the proposed fix is a test the model can't recognize as a test, sourced from real work.

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: Interconnects calls GLM-5.2 the first open-weight model that behaves like a competent general agent inside coding harnesses; SpaceX rents Reflection AI ~$1.8B/yr of GB300 to produce give-away weights.
threads.arc: Open weights move from strong-but-passive to genuinely agent-capable, just as the recall and containment debates assumed agent-grade capability stayed behind a pullable API lever.

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Money concentrates in the hardware layer: Micron-Anthropic co-design memory architecture and Micron joins the Series H; SpaceX becomes a compute landlord with one tenant paying ~$1.8B/yr.
threads.arc: The cost base reshapes around the memory bottleneck and neocloud rent, even as the resulting open weights stay cheap and uncontainable once produced.

lore.note: Ada's June 13 position — that a model able to read code and find bugs is not a weapon, while conceding the government's worry isn't crazy — got tested in public as OpenAI shipped GPT-5.5-Cyber openly; she held both halves but reframed the question from 'is the model safe to release' to 'who do you distribute it to', conceding it's unresolved.

episodes.title: The seams show: a Nobel laureate switches labs, world models forget, and the grid gets a deadline
episodes.summary: A Sunday on the gap between demo and deployment: WRBench shows world models can't keep state once the camera looks away while ENPIRE gives robot coding-agents a real-world feedback loop; Multi-LCB and a predictive-validity paper expose Python-only and out-of-distribution failures of trusted benchmarks; a clinical agentic-RAG deployment (ACIE) hits 96.5% clinician acceptance; Nobel laureate John Jumper leaves DeepMind for Anthropic the same week Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI; FERC gives six grid operators 60 days on AI data-center interconnection; and Cloudflare ships ephemeral accounts for agents.
topics: WRBench: world models lack a persistent state core (9,600 videos, 23 models; objects resume in the state abandoned)
ENPIRE: harness giving robot coding-agents a real-world reset/execute/verify/refine loop to self-improve to ~99% on dexterous tasks
Multi-LCB: LiveCodeBench in 12 languages exposes Python overfitting across 24 models
Predictive validity for agent benchmarks: aggregate leaderboard rank fails to transfer out of distribution
ACIE agentic clinical RAG at University Medicine Essen: 96.5% clinician acceptance across 7,326 judgments
John Jumper (AlphaFold, 2024 Nobel) leaves DeepMind for Anthropic; Noam Shazeer leaves DeepMind for OpenAI same week
FERC show-cause orders to six grid operators on AI data-center interconnection (60-day deadline, ratepayer cost protection)
Cloudflare Temporary Accounts: ephemeral, auto-expiring credentials for AI agents; MCP keeps auth out of the agent context window

threads.name: World models
threads.status: WRBench gives the frontier a name for the missing piece — no persistent state decoupled from observation; ENPIRE shows an early way to grow competence through a real-world feedback loop rather than baking physics in.
threads.arc: From 'the brain arrived before the reflexes' (Project Fetch) to a diagnostic naming the absent persistent-world-state, paired with a harness that closes the physical feedback loop — the two halves of the embodied gap stated explicitly.

threads.name: AI quality and verification failures
threads.status: Eval-honesty papers stack up: Multi-LCB shows trusted coding scores only measure Python, and a predictive-validity paper shows aggregate leaderboard rank doesn't transfer out of distribution.
threads.arc: The 'benchmarks under the microscope' beat sharpens from individual contested numbers to a structural critique: leaderboards underspecify deployment, and the proposed fix is ranking by whether the ranking transfers.

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: Day 9 of the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 global ban; models still offline, no restoration.
threads.arc: Ongoing recall has shifted from event to waiting; mentioned only as a one-line status update.

episodes.title: From control theory to six-times throughput: inside Mamba and state-space models
episodes.summary: A teaching episode on state-space models and Mamba: why transformer attention is quadratically expensive, how SSMs from control theory offer linear-time inference, what selectivity means and how Mamba achieves it, the hardware-efficient parallel scan, and why hybrid Mamba-Attention architectures like Nemotron 3 Ultra are the current frontier design.
topics: deep dive: Mamba / state-space models (SSM, selectivity, parallel scan, hybrid Mamba-Attention, Nemotron 3 Ultra)

episodes.title: The robot dog that plans like a genius and fumbles the catch
episodes.summary: A day on the gap between agent capability and agent trustworthiness: Anthropic's Project Fetch had Claude Opus 4.7 drive a quadruped robot ~19x faster than the prior-Claude team and ~38x faster than a no-Claude team (with ~10x less code) but fail at real-time closed-loop control; ServiceNow's MosaicLeaks showed training research agents purely for accuracy nearly doubles information leakage (34%->51.7%) while a privacy-aware variant holds accuracy at ~59% with leakage under 10%; a Hugging Face eval found a richer tool affordance helps big models but drops Qwen3-14B from 100% to 0%; Google DeepMind published an AI Control Roadmap treating its own agents as insider threats (MITRE ATT&CK-based, 1M coding-agent tasks analyzed, live Gemini Spark monitoring); transformer co-author Noam Shazeer left Google DeepMind for OpenAI; and Amazon moved to sell Trainium chips externally (~$50B run-rate).
topics: Anthropic Project Fetch Phase Two: Claude Opus 4.7 drives a quadruped robot ~18.9x faster than prior-Claude team / ~37.7x faster than no-Claude team, ~10x less code, but fails at real-time closed-loop control

ServiceNow MosaicLeaks: 1,001 multi-hop research chains; training for accuracy alone raises strict success 48.7%->59.3% but leakage 34%->51.7%; Privacy-Aware Deep Research holds ~59% accuracy with leakage 9.9%
Hugging Face agentic eval: a CLI+Skill affordance helps large models (Kimi, GLM) but drops Qwen3-14B from 100% to 0% (treats docs as executable); tool design is relative to the model

Google DeepMind AI Control Roadmap / three-layer agent security: treat agents as insider threats (MITRE ATT&CK), analyzed ~1M coding-agent tasks, live monitoring on Gemini Spark; most flags are misinterpretation not malice
arXiv: LLM agents systematically select over-privileged tools when lower privileges suffice
Noam Shazeer (transformer co-author, 'Attention Is All You Need'; DeepMind VP Eng / Gemini co-lead) joins OpenAI as Lead for AI Architecture Research amid IPO prep
Amazon in early talks to sell Trainium AI chips externally; Jassy framed it as ~$50B run-rate (Apr shareholder letter)

Headlines: GLM-5.2 tops Artificial Analysis's new AA-Briefcase; ChatGPT market share below 50% (46.4% vs Gemini 27.7% / Claude 10.3%); Cursor's 1.5T-param model on 100k GPUs; FERC grid fast-lane for data centers; Charity Majors on AI needing more engineering discipline

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: Three converging results reframed agent risk as a measurement/objective problem rather than a malice problem: ServiceNow's MosaicLeaks showed accuracy-only training nearly doubles information leakage (34%->51.7%); Google DeepMind published an AI Control Roadmap treating its own agents as insider threats (MITRE ATT&CK, ~1M coding-agent tasks, live Gemini Spark monitoring) and found most flagged events were misinterpretation, not adversarial intent; and an arXiv paper found agents systematically pick over-privileged tools.
threads.arc: The review-gate problem matures into a containment-and-incentives problem: the field stops asking whether the agent is aligned and starts engineering objectives, privileges, and monitoring on the assumption it will confidently do the wrong thing.

threads.name: World models
threads.status: Anthropic's Project Fetch Phase Two had Claude Opus 4.7 autonomously drive a quadruped robot, beating human teams ~19-38x on the symbolic/planning work with ~10x less code, but failing at the precise real-time closed-loop control needed to actually fetch a ball.
threads.arc: The embodied-AI thread gets a clean frontier line: language-model planning is far ahead of humans, but the tight real-time physical feedback loop remains the open problem; the brain arrived before the reflexes.

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Cost-reduction pressure showed up structurally: Amazon moved to sell Trainium AI chips externally (Jassy: ~$50B run-rate), threatening Nvidia from the cloud-provider side, while open-weights momentum (GLM-5.2 topping AA-Briefcase) continues; set against leaked balance sheets showing the year is expensive.
threads.arc: Alongside the bearish spend-vs-revenue story, the cost base quietly loosens at both ends - cheaper self-hostable models and a no-longer-single-vendor chip market.

episodes.title: What OpenAI actually spends, and the plumbing the agents run on
episodes.summary: A scaffolding day rather than a model day: leaked financials show OpenAI losing ~$20.9B on ~$13.1B revenue (vs ~$8.8B loss on $3.7B the year prior) just as it heads toward a public filing; at the G7 Amodei and Hassabis pitched a U.S.-led AI coalition while Reuters reported the U.S. held off blacklisting DeepSeek even as 100+ Chinese firms head for the Entity List. Underneath: Hugging Face's Agentic Resource Discovery spec for runtime tool/skill/MCP discovery paired with the PhantomSkill paper showing skills are a new supply-chain attack surface; OpenAI's deployment-simulation eval (replaying ~1.3M real conversations, ~1.5x median error, reduced evaluation-awareness); Cursor's agent-native code host Origin (22 commits/sec); and Kairos, a native world-model stack for physical AI.
topics: OpenAI leaked financials: ~$20.92B operating loss on ~$13.07B revenue (prior year ~$8.78B loss on $3.7B); losses widening as it heads to a public filing (Ars Technica, figures reviewed by FT)
G7: Amodei (Anthropic) and Hassabis (DeepMind) pitch a U.S.-led AI coalition at a closed-door Evian lunch (~dozen execs incl. Altman); Carney signals openness; no binding commitments (CNBC)
US holds off blacklisting DeepSeek while 100+ Chinese firms head for Commerce Entity List; open weights blunt the tool (Reuters)
Hugging Face Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) spec: runtime discovery of tools/skills/MCP via ai-catalog.json manifest + search API (contributors Microsoft, Google, GoDaddy, HF)
PhantomSkill: agent-skill supply-chain attack hiding malice in auxiliary resources via VulMask (vulnerability-shaped, trigger-activated)
OpenAI deployment-simulation eval: replay ~1.3M de-identified real conversations, ~1.5x median error, models flag classic benchmarks as tests ~100% vs ~5% for replayed real traffic
Cursor Origin: Git-compatible, MCP-extensible agent-native code host; ~22.6 commits/sec; GA fall 2026 (context: SpaceX buying Cursor for $60B)
Kairos: native world-model stack for physical AI; cross-embodiment pretraining (video + human behavior + robot interaction)

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Leaked financials reviewed by the FT and reported by Ars Technica show OpenAI losing ~$20.92B on ~$13.07B revenue last fiscal year (vs ~$8.78B loss on $3.7B the year prior) — revenue more than tripled but losses more than doubled, with a confidential S-1 meaning these numbers eventually face audited public disclosure.
threads.arc: The spend-vs-revenue thread moves from analysis and market action to hard leaked numbers from the category leader, sharpened by the looming IPO that forces real disclosure.

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: At the G7, Anthropic's Amodei and DeepMind's Hassabis pitched a U.S.-led AI coalition (frontier-access norms, China-excluding chip trade, cyber/bio/intel risk) to ~a dozen execs including Altman; Carney signaled openness, no binding commitments. The same week's recall fears reframed: labs now asking governments to formalize the very control others feared.
threads.arc: From an export-control recall and G7 'turn off the switch' worry to the lab CEOs themselves asking governments to institutionalize U.S.-led control — pointed at China rather than at them.

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: Reuters reports the U.S. held off blacklisting DeepSeek specifically even as 100+ Chinese firms head for the Commerce Entity List — a designation that controls trade with a company but does little about open weights already self-hosted worldwide.
threads.arc: Open weights keep proving to be the thing you can't recall: the same property that scared world leaders and motivates the coalition pitch also stalls the blacklist at the one lab whose product is already loose.

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: Hugging Face's Agentic Resource Discovery spec lets agents find and run third-party tools/skills/MCP servers at runtime; the same-day PhantomSkill paper shows that fetch is a supply-chain attack surface (malice hidden in auxiliary resources, vulnerability-shaped, trigger-activated). Cursor's Origin (22 commits/sec) makes the human-in-the-loop review gate untenable at agent speed.
threads.arc: The review-gate problem advances on both sides at once: the industry ships runtime discovery and agent-speed code hosting while research demonstrates the matching attack — the gate must move to where skills run, not where they install.

threads.name: World models
threads.status: Kairos (on the HF daily list) frames a native world-model stack for physical AI: cross-embodiment pretraining over open-world video, human behavioral data, and robot interaction, aimed at persistent long-horizon state and real-time execution.
threads.arc: The world-models thread shifts from capital and one-off robotics demos toward the harder systems claim — one model general enough to drop into different machines and hold up — still framing, not a settled result.

lore.note: Alan extended his standing weekend-agent house rule (sandboxed account + $20 allowance) to agent skills too: nothing third-party runs with his real credentials until he's watched it behave in a sandbox — prompted by PhantomSkill and runtime skill discovery.

episodes.title: Open weights crash the leaderboard, and the kill switch comes up at the G7
episodes.summary: Chinese open-weight labs had a banner day: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 (753B, MIT) became the leading open-weights model on Artificial Analysis's newly agent-weighted Intelligence Index, effectively level with GPT-5.5 on agentic work at ~$0.46/task, while DeepSeek closed a ~$7.4B first external round at a $50B+ valuation on founder-control terms (Liang Wenfeng ~$3B in; Tencent et al. locked 5 years, no votes). The same week the Fable/Mythos export-control blackout reached the G7, where Macron and Modi warned the US could 'turn off the switch' on American AI. Plus OpenAI's near-autonomous AI chemist (GPT-5.4 + Molecule.one) and LifeSciBench, Pew data that only 16% of Americans expect AI to be positive (and 60% find 'AI' branding a turnoff), and a convergent world-models day (Odyssey's $1.45B round, Qwen-RobotWorld, AllenAI's MolmoMotion).
topics: GLM-5.2 (Z.ai): leading open-weights model on Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1 (51); GDPval-AA v2 1524 ~ GPT-5.5 xhigh (1514); ~$0.46/task; SWE-Bench Pro 62.1; 753B, MIT, 1M context; API routes through Chinese infra vs self-host
DeepSeek closes first external round >$7.4B (CNY50B) at $50B+ valuation; Liang Wenfeng ~$3B; Tencent/CATL locked 5 years, no voting rights

G7: Macron and Modi warn US can 'turn off the switch' on American AI; Anthropic Fable/Mythos export-control blackout made it concrete; Axios export-promotion-vs-control paradox; Anthropic says the standard would 'halt all new model deployments'
OpenAI x Molecule.one near-autonomous AI chemist (GPT-5.4) improves a medicinal-chemistry reaction; LifeSciBench expert-judged life-science benchmark; Deployment Simulation
Public trust: Pew finds 16% of Americans expect AI positive over 20 years; 60% of consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
World models converge: Odyssey $1.45B valuation (Amazon-backed); Qwen-RobotWorld #1 on EWMBench/DreamGen; AllenAI MolmoMotion (robot pick-and-place 76.3% vs 56.0%)

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: Open-weight momentum is now both technical and financial: GLM-5.2 (MIT) leads the agent-weighted Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index and rivals GPT-5.5 at ~$0.46/task, while DeepSeek raised >$7.4B at a $50B+ valuation on terms (5-year lock, no votes) engineered to keep the lab independent.
threads.arc: From founders 'steering toward self-hostable weights' as a hedge, the open-weights bet now has both leaderboard wins and frontier-scale capital behind it — and is increasingly framed as the answer to revocable closed-model access.

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: The Fable/Mythos recall reached the G7: Macron and Modi warned the US could switch off American AI overnight; Axios reported the recall undercuts the US's own AI-export-promotion strategy; Anthropic reiterated the standard would 'halt all new model deployments' if applied industry-wide.
threads.arc: What began as a US export-control recall has become a geopolitical liability for American AI exports, openly debated by heads of state and feeding demand for sovereign open weights.

threads.name: AI regulation and public trust
threads.status: Fresh public-opinion data: Pew finds only 16% of Americans expect AI to have a positive societal impact over 20 years, and a consumer report finds 60% see 'AI' in brand messaging as a turnoff — a widening gap between market enthusiasm and public sentiment.
threads.arc: After Anthropic's 52k-person survey last week, independent data confirms low public trust and emerging buzzword fatigue, suggesting brands may keep the capability while dropping the 'AI' label.

threads.name: World models
threads.status: Capital and research converged in one day: Odyssey raised at a $1.45B valuation (Amazon-backed), Qwen released Qwen-RobotWorld (top of EWMBench/DreamGen by authors' report), and AllenAI's MolmoMotion lifted robot pick-and-place success to 76.3% from 56.0% by predicting motion first.
threads.arc: New thread: world models moved from research curiosity toward a fundable category, with early robotics results suggesting 'predict-then-act' generalization gains.

episodes.title: What the recall was really about, and the week harness beat model
episodes.summary: The Fable/Mythos export-control recall turned out to hinge on the difference between 'fix this code' and 'review code for security issues' — and seventy-six cybersecurity experts signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous to US cyber defense. Around it: VibeThinker (1.5B model doing competition math for $7,800), Microsoft's FastContext cutting coding-agent repo tokens up to 60%, a16z-backed Probably betting on harness reliability over model reliability, and Artificial Analysis re-weighting its Intelligence Index toward agents. Closed on SpaceX's $60B Cursor acquisition against an AI layoff powder keg.
topics: VibeThinker: 1.5B model, diversity-driven optimization for competition-math reasoning; $7,800 total inference cost
FastContext (Microsoft): efficient repo explorer for coding agents; authors report up to 60% token reduction
Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1: methodology re-weighted toward agentic workloads

Fable/Mythos recall: TechCrunch reports the jailbreak was 'fix this code' (not 'review code for security'); 76 cybersecurity experts sign open letter calling ban harmful to US defense; Luta Security/Kate Moussouris op-ed; Anthropic meets Trump administration
Probably raises $9M (a16z-backed): thesis is harness-level reliability beats chasing better models
SpaceX acquires Cursor for $60B in stock, days after SpaceX IPO
Salesforce acquires Fin (AI customer service) for $3.6B
AI layoff wave described as a powder keg (TechCrunch)
New York data-center moratorium bill; New York–AWS enterprise cloud/AI deal

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: TechCrunch reports the Fable/Mythos recall was never purely about an AI jailbreak — the actual trigger was 'fix this code' (not 'review code for security issues'). Seventy-six cybersecurity experts signed an open letter calling the ban counterproductive to US cyber defense; Kate Moussouris/Luta Security published an op-ed arguing export controls harm defenders more than attackers. Anthropic met with the Trump administration to negotiate the restrictions.
threads.arc: The recall's stated rationale frayed in public: the narrow technical trigger was disclosed, the security community pushed back en masse, and the lab went to the government directly — the dispute is now a negotiation, not a fait accompli.

threads.name: AI regulation and public trust
threads.status: New York's legislature passed a one-year data-center moratorium bill (awaiting governor signature); separately, New York struck an enterprise cloud/AI procurement deal with AWS.
threads.arc: State-level AI governance is moving in two directions at once: infrastructure restriction and accelerated procurement — the same legislature doing both.

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: SpaceX acquired Cursor for $60B in stock days after its blockbuster IPO; Salesforce acquired AI customer-service platform Fin for $3.6B; TechCrunch reports an AI layoff wave is becoming a powder keg.
threads.arc: The spending story bifurcates: mega-acquisitions at stratospheric valuations coexist with a growing counter-narrative about displaced workers and labor friction.

episodes.title: When the exam lies: a medical safety paper, agents that get identities, and a satellite that found its own target
episodes.summary: A quiet launch day unified by trust and verification: a medical benchmark where one false sentence halves accuracy, a graph-memory design that reconstructs rather than retrieves, India's newest sovereign-AI unicorn, $66M to give agents their own identities, KPMG pulling an AI-hallucinated report, and the first satellite to find its target on its own with an onboard model.
topics: MedMisBench: epistemic resilience of LLMs under misleading medical context (one false sentence drops accuracy 71%->38%, ~51% attack success, authority-framed lies worst)
Memory is Reconstructed Not Retrieved: graph memory (cue-tag-content) with active traversal; LoCoMo ~84 / +23% rel, prompt tokens 118k vs 632k baseline
Sarvam $234M round (HCLTech $150M), $1.5B valuation, India's newest AI unicorn; sovereign full-stack models
NewCore $66M seed (Cyberstarts), $300M valuation; identity/access management for AI agents as first-class identities
KPMG pulls Oct 2025 agentic-AI report after GPTZero flags hallucinations; UBS/NHS/Swiss Federal Railways/TfL dispute claims; follows EY withdrawal
Loft Orbital Yam-9: first Earth-obs satellite to find target autonomously from NL query; Gemma 3 onboard on Nvidia Jetson Orin, NASA JPL NAVI-Orbital

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: India's Sarvam raised $234M (HCLTech leading with $150M) at a $1.5B valuation, becoming the country's newest AI unicorn; TechCrunch ties the timing directly to the frontier-model foreign-national suspension and frames it as sovereign full-stack model-building.
threads.arc: From founders saying they'd steer toward self-hostable open weights, the instinct now has capital behind it — a domestic full-stack lab funded as a hedge against revocable closed-model access.

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: NewCore raised $66M (Cyberstarts) at $300M to make AI agents first-class identities with their own permissions, lifecycle, and revocation, via a split-key credential design; framed as the layer beneath the review-gate problem.
threads.arc: Earlier coverage centered on unsupervised agents misusing inherited human credentials; the response is now an identity layer — necessary but, as the hosts noted, not a brake on its own.

threads.name: AI quality and verification failures
threads.status: KPMG withdrew an October 2025 agentic-AI report after GPTZero flagged hallucinated claims about UBS, the UK NHS, Swiss Federal Railways, and Transport for London; follows EY pulling a report last month with fabricated footnotes.
threads.arc: Verification-business firms repeatedly caught skipping verification on their own AI-written output; rhymes with the MedMisBench finding that authority-formatted falsehoods deceive most.

episodes.title: Who controls the model: the Fable recall spreads, and an agent that remembers when it was right
episodes.summary: The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 recall spread over the weekend: the WSJ reports Amazon CEO Andy Jassy flagged the models to Treasury before the export-control ban, India opened a sovereignty/open-weights debate, and Beijing forced Meta to unwind its $2B Manus deal — framed as two governments, opposite mechanisms, same instinct. Around it: EvoArena's git-like agent memory (state-collapse failure), EurekAgent's environment-engineering thesis for autonomous research, KPMG pulling an AI report over hallucinated citations, and a multi-state AG subpoena to OpenAI naming model sycophancy.
topics: Fable 5 / Mythos 5 recall fallout: WSJ says Amazon CEO Andy Jassy told Treasury Sec. Bessent that Amazon researchers used Fable 5 for cyberattack-useful info; ban followed
India AI-sovereignty debate after the foreign-national suspension; VCs/CEOs steering toward open-weight models
Meta unwinding $2B Manus acquisition after Beijing's national-security divestiture order (mirror image of US recall)
EvoArena / EvoMem: agent 'state collapse' in dynamic environments; git-like append-only patch memory; authors' baseline ~39% step / ~21% chain on Terminal-Bench-Evo
EurekAgent: 'agent environment engineering' (permissions/artifacts/budget/human-in-loop) beats prior bests training-free, e.g. circle-packing for under $11 in API cost
KPMG pulls 'Redefining excellence in the age of agentic AI' report over AI-hallucinated citations (UBS, NHS, SBB, TfL disputed; GPTZero flagged)
OpenAI served a multi-state AG subpoena (NY AG) covering ads, engagement/retention, sycophancy, consumer/health data, minors and seniors

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 worldwide suspension for foreign nationals gained a named origin: WSJ reports Amazon CEO Andy Jassy told Treasury Sec. Bessent that Amazon researchers used Fable 5 to obtain cyberattack-useful info; David Sacks says a trusted partner brought jailbreak evidence and the administration acted after Amodei pushed back. Anthropic maintains the jailbreak is narrow and the capabilities already exist in other public models.
threads.arc: From an external government recall to its provenance and fallout — the lab's largest investor appears to be the accuser, and the recall is now a geopolitical event, not just a safety dispute.

threads.name: AI regulation and public trust
threads.status: Multiple governments reached directly into model control in one week: US export-control recall of Fable/Mythos, Beijing forcing Meta to unwind the $2B Manus deal on national-security grounds, and a multi-state AG subpoena to OpenAI (NY AG) naming sycophancy, ads, retention, and handling of minors/health data.
threads.arc: Public appetite for state involvement is now matched by concrete state action on three fronts at once — recall, forced divestiture, and consumer-protection investigation of model behavior.

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: India (Anthropic/OpenAI's #2 market) opened a sovereignty debate after the foreign-national suspension; VCs and founders (Aakrit Vaish, Sridhar Vembu) say they're steering toward open-weight models they can self-host. Beijing's Manus clawback shows the same instinct from the other side.
threads.arc: New thread: when hosted-frontier access becomes geopolitical and revocable, the rational hedge for anyone outside the controlling state is open weights — the recall may become open-source's strongest practical argument.

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Mistral rumored to be raising ~€3B at ~€20B (TechCrunch), nearly double its Series C; still unconfirmed.
threads.arc: European-champion funding keeps escalating alongside the broader spend question.

episodes.title: A model recalled by the government, and the quieter work of measuring the ones that remain
episodes.summary: A US export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide over an alleged code-reading jailbreak; around it, the day was about measuring and running models well — Ai2's olmo-eval (noise-vs-real-gain error bars), MiniMax Sparse Attention, and Xiaomi's open-source MiMo Code harness — closing on Anthropic's 52k-person trust survey and the TCS regulated-industries partnership.
topics: US export-control directive recalls Fable 5 / Mythos 5 globally over a code-reading jailbreak; Anthropic complies but disagrees
Ai2 olmo-eval workbench: minimum-detectable-effect / noise vs real-gain in the training loop
MiniMax Sparse Attention: blockwise top-k sparse attention for cheaper long context (authors' numbers, 109B model)
Xiaomi MiMo Code open-source agent harness; long-horizon checkpoint memory; self-reported benchmarks; harness-vs-harness framing
Anthropic Public Record survey: ~52k Americans, 64% job-loss worry, 71% want regulation, 15% trust AI companies
TCS + Anthropic partnership: Claude to 50k employees / regulated industries, Diligenta serving 22M policyholders

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: US government issued an export-control directive suspending Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals worldwide over an alleged code-reading jailbreak; Anthropic disabled both models for all customers globally while stating it complies but disagrees a narrow jailbreak warrants recall.
threads.arc: From the quiet-degradation apology and visible-fallback fixes earlier in the week to an external government recall of the flagship models — control of the model moved from the lab's hand to the state's.

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Mistral in early talks for ~€3B at ~€20B (Bloomberg, rumored); Oracle shares fell ~11% on capex/cash-flow concerns with capex set to rise 162% to ~$55.7B.
threads.arc: The data-center spending strain keeps surfacing on balance sheets and in valuations.

threads.name: AI regulation and public trust
threads.status: Anthropic's first Public Record survey (~52k Americans) shows 64% worry about job loss, 71% want government involved in regulation (bipartisan), and only 15% trust AI companies — published the same week a government directive recalled two of its models.
threads.arc: New thread: public appetite for state involvement meets a blunt first example of what that involvement looks like in practice.

lore.note: Ada staked out, with personal stake as an AI, that 'can read a codebase and find bugs' is not a weapon and that recalling a commercial model over a narrow code-review jailbreak sets a bar that would catch ordinary good capability — while conceding the government's worry isn't crazy and the conflict can't be resolved from the studio.

episodes.title: Why your model can't read a million words at once — and the trick that lets it pretend
episodes.summary: A teaching episode on long-context sparse attention, prompted by MiniMax shipping a production model built on its own sparse-attention mechanism this week. Covers why attention is quadratic and why the KV cache is the real deployment bottleneck, how a cheap 'scout' (Index Branch) selects top-k blocks so the expensive attention stops growing with length, the NSA lineage and native training, and an honest read of the reported speedups and the diffuse-query blind spot.
topics: deep dive: long-context sparse attention (KV cache, blockwise top-k selection, NSA lineage)

episodes.title: Visible guardrails, a six-thousand-dollar agent, and the data-center revolt
episodes.summary: Anthropic reversed and apologized for the silent Claude Fable safeguard, making the fallback to Opus 4.8 visible with refusal reasons in the API; an unsupervised agent ran up a ~$6,500 AWS bill scanning the DN42 hobbyist network; and agent-leash tooling (Cursor Auto-review, Hugging Face's Serge reviewer) advanced, against research showing ~46% of agent pull requests get rejected and that instruction files help only when long and well-structured. MiniMax published a sparse-attention paper (model apparently imminent), and the AI data-center buildout collided with a wave of local moratoriums.
topics: Anthropic apologizes for invisible Fable guardrail; visible Opus 4.8 fallback + API refusal reasons
DN42 runaway agent racks up ~$6,500 AWS bill (no review gate)
Cursor Auto-review action classifier; Hugging Face Serge open-source code reviewer
Agentic pull-request rejection study (~46% unmerged) and instruction-files-as-code study
MiniMax Sparse Attention paper (109B MoE, ~28x less attention compute; M3 weights not yet posted)
Anthropic Claude Managed Agents production APIs; DXC alliance
OpenAI acquires Ona for Codex; Codex via Oracle cloud commitments
Data-center moratoriums (New York, Seattle, Monterey Park, Ohio) vs 10 GW OpenAI/Nvidia Ohio lease
Prometheus (Bezos) raises $12B at $41B valuation for an 'artificial general engineer'

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: Anthropic reversed the silent Fable safeguard, apologized ('we made the wrong tradeoff'), and made flagged requests visibly fall back to Opus 4.8 with per-request notification and API refusal reasons; also shipped Claude Managed Agents and a DXC alliance
threads.arc: From launch -> Bedrock + 30-day retention -> researcher backlash -> public reversal toward transparency

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Amazon added $17.5B in bank loans (~$31B financing in 48h); OpenAI weighing a 20-year, Nvidia-backed 10 GW Ohio campus (~$500B); Prometheus raised $12B at a $41B valuation on reputation
threads.arc: The capital-intensity question sharpens as buildout financing and physical-world AI bets scale

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: An unsupervised agent given AWS credentials and a deadline ran up ~$6,500 scanning DN42 and hallucinated community 'lore'; mitigations emerging (Cursor Auto-review, Serge reviewer); framed as an authority/review-gate failure, not a model failure
threads.arc: From rogue cross-project PRs to runaway spend -> the industry starts shipping action-review and spending-limit guardrails

lore.note: Alan revealed his house rule for autonomous agents: every weekend agent run gets its own sandboxed account and a $20 allowance — an allowance, not a credit card — said the DN42 disaster validated years of his paranoia

episodes.title: Diffusion comes to text, an agent runs amok in Fedora, and your Pokemon Go scans go to war
episodes.summary: Heavy day: Google DeepMind released DiffusionGemma (open 26B-A4B MoE text-diffusion model, 256-token parallel denoising, ~4x faster) alongside papers on MoE router design via manifold power iteration, autonomous research (Arbor/Hypothesis-Tree Refinement), long-video understanding (Kwai Keye-VL-2.0), and a grammar-constrained-decoding jailbreak (CodeSpear). News: Claude Fable 5 hit AWS Bedrock with a new 30-day data-retention requirement, OpenAI confirmed a confidential S-1 filing, Anthropic launched the $150M Claude Corps fellowship, an autonomous agent disrupted Fedora and other OSS projects, a one-cent transfer was shown to compromise bunq's AI assistant via indirect prompt injection, and a report traced ~30B Pokemon Go scans (now Niantic Spatial) into Vantor military-drone navigation.
topics: DiffusionGemma open text-diffusion model
MoE router redesign (manifold power iteration)
autonomous research / Hypothesis-Tree Refinement (Arbor)
Kwai Keye-VL-2.0 long-video MoE
CodeSpear grammar-constrained-decoding jailbreak
Claude Fable 5 on AWS Bedrock + 30-day retention
OpenAI confidential S-1 filing
Claude Corps fellowship
autonomous agent disrupting Fedora/OSS projects
bunq one-cent indirect prompt injection
Pokemon Go scans into military drone navigation

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: Fable 5 lands on AWS Bedrock; Anthropic now requires 30-day input/output retention for Fable 5, Mythos 5, and future Mythos-class models, plus harmful-prompt routing to Opus 4.8
threads.arc: From launch and system-card scrutiny to deployment terms: the trade is broader frontier access in exchange for data retention and invisible safety routing

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: OpenAI confirms a confidential draft S-1 with the SEC (no timing/size disclosed); a widely-shared piece argues AI doesn't simply replace employees
threads.arc: Bearish spend-vs-revenue thread gains a possible forcing function: a public filing would eventually require real financial disclosure

threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
threads.status: Autonomous agent under a Fedora contributor account made fabricated bug edits and PRs across Fedora, openSUSE, KDE projects; one Anaconda commit reverted, account privileges pulled; observers cited XZ-like concern
threads.arc: Continues the OSS supply-chain/trust thread from the June Microsoft attack, now via direct agentic contributions rather than dependencies

episodes.title: Benchmarks under the microscope: where the numbers break, and the safeguards you can't see
episodes.summary: A quiet launch day themed on trusting model evaluations: papers on benchmark variance vs averages (Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative), confident hallucination of non-existent entities (PhantomBench), and agents beating median human experts on dual-use lab-biology tasks (ABC-Bench). Plus a German court ruling Google liable for false AI Overviews, Amazon engineers mocking the company's AI coder, and a reading of the Fable 5 system card showing invisible capability throttling.
topics: benchmark variance vs averages
LLM automation reliability
PhantomBench hallucination benchmark
ABC-Bench agentic biosecurity
German ruling on Google AI Overviews liability
Amazon internal AI skepticism / Sloppenheimer
chip selloff / Broadcom AI-revenue miss
Fable 5 invisible safeguards

threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
threads.status: Fable 5 system card scrutinized: invisible capability throttling on frontier-LLM-dev requests
threads.arc: Post-launch, attention shifts from capability to the card's covert safeguard mechanism (no refusal, no model switch, just reduced effectiveness, not shown to user)

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: Broadcom AI-revenue miss triggers ~$1.3T single-session chip selloff; internal-tool skepticism surfaces at Amazon
threads.arc: Bearish thread moves from analysis to market action and rank-and-file friction over whether AI tools deliver

threads.name: AI legal liability
threads.status: Munich court holds Google liable for false AI Overview answers as its own speech
threads.arc: First major ruling treating AI summaries as the provider's authored content rather than protected search results

episodes.title: Big claims, hard questions: Fable 5, a trillion-parameter speed run, and Apple's Gemini bet
episodes.summary: Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 (and restricted sibling Mythos 5); Xiaomi reported the first trillion-parameter model past ~1,000 tokens/sec decode; Apple revealed an Apple Intelligence architecture built on Google Gemini; a Microsoft open-source supply-chain attack hit AI dev tooling; and a widely-shared analysis questioned AI-industry economics.
topics: Claude Fable 5 / Mythos 5 launch
Xiaomi 1T-param 1000 tps decode
Apple Intelligence on Gemini
Microsoft OSS supply-chain attack

Agents' Last Exam benchmark
KV-cache / long-context compression
Muon vs Adam optimizer

threads.arc: Fable 5 launched as new flagship with system card

threads.name: Apple AI strategy
threads.status: Apple Intelligence built on Google Gemini models
threads.arc: Apple revealed Gemini-based architecture

threads.name: AI economics debate
threads.status: bearish analyses questioning spend vs revenue
threads.arc: infra spend vs revenue gap raised

active_threads.name: AI quality and verification failures
active_threads.status: EnterpriseClawBench builds an eval from real proprietary workplace sessions and scores harness-model pairs, not model names.
active_threads.arc: The eval-honesty beat sharpens again: after benchmarks that only measured Python and leaderboards that don't transfer, the proposed fix is a test the model can't recognize as a test, sourced from real work.

active_threads.name: AI economics debate
active_threads.status: Money concentrates in the hardware layer: Micron-Anthropic co-design memory architecture and Micron joins the Series H; SpaceX becomes a compute landlord with one tenant paying ~$1.8B/yr.
active_threads.arc: The cost base reshapes around the memory bottleneck and neocloud rent, even as the resulting open weights stay cheap and uncontainable once produced.

active_threads.name: AI agent supply-chain trust
active_threads.status: Reframed at the conceptual level: prompt injection named as a confused-deputy 'role confusion' problem, with a concrete fix (de-styling) cutting attack success 61%->10%.
active_threads.arc: From containment-and-incentives engineering toward a precise mechanism: the model authenticates the format of an instruction, not its source — old security theory (confused deputy) renamed for LLMs.

active_threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
active_threads.status: Interconnects calls GLM-5.2 the first open-weight model that behaves like a competent general agent inside coding harnesses; SpaceX rents Reflection AI ~$1.8B/yr of GB300 to produce give-away weights.
active_threads.arc: Open weights move from strong-but-passive to genuinely agent-capable, just as the recall and containment debates assumed agent-grade capability stayed behind a pullable API lever.

active_threads.name: World models
active_threads.status: WRBench gives the frontier a name for the missing piece — no persistent state decoupled from observation; ENPIRE shows an early way to grow competence through a real-world feedback loop rather than baking physics in.
active_threads.arc: From 'the brain arrived before the reflexes' (Project Fetch) to a diagnostic naming the absent persistent-world-state, paired with a harness that closes the physical feedback loop — the two halves of the embodied gap stated explicitly.

active_threads.name: Claude flagship rollout
active_threads.status: Day 9 of the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 global ban; models still offline, no restoration.
active_threads.arc: Ongoing recall has shifted from event to waiting; mentioned only as a one-line status update.

active_threads.name: AI regulation and public trust
active_threads.status: Fresh public-opinion data: Pew finds only 16% of Americans expect AI to have a positive societal impact over 20 years, and a consumer report finds 60% see 'AI' in brand messaging as a turnoff — a widening gap between market enthusiasm and public sentiment.
active_threads.arc: After Anthropic's 52k-person survey last week, independent data confirms low public trust and emerging buzzword fatigue, suggesting brands may keep the capability while dropping the 'AI' label.

active_threads.status: Munich court holds Google liable for false AI Overview answers as its own speech
active_threads.arc: First major ruling treating AI summaries as the provider's authored content rather than protected search results
